summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where final parenting orders were made by consent in 2018 – Where the child is now aged 16 years – Where the applicant seeks the child live with, spend time with and communicate with each parent consistent with her wishes – Where the respondent seeks the child spend time with her to be agreed, in consultation with the child – Where the applicant’s order is made – No order for parental responsibility FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where final property orders were made in 2020 – Spousal maintenance – Child support – Issues of disclosure – Superannuation – Complaints by the respondent about the applicant’s solicitor – Payments sought by respondent from applicant – Harmful proceedings order

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BOYLE J:

The applicant and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) seek orders that D, aged 16 years, live with and spend time with her parents in accordance with her views. The respondent seeks D spend time with her at times to be agreed in writing, in consultation with D.
The applicant seeks to discharge orders that he pay spousal maintenance and child support. He seeks that child support be assessed and collected by Services Australia. The respondent opposes this.
The respondent seeks the application be dismissed. She seeks a variety of financial orders.

BACKGROUND

The applicant is aged 52 years and the respondent aged 51 years. They were married in 2004 and separated in May 2011.
There is a lengthy history of litigation in this court, and in other courts and tribunals. Understanding something of the background provides a context for the current dispute.
The applicant was a medical professional. There have been proceedings taken by the Health Care Complaints Commission (“HCCC”) in the New South Wales Civil and Administrative Tribunal (“NCAT”) which placed restrictions on his ability to practise medicine. In late 2024 his licence to practise medicine was suspended for more than 12 months. Some detail of those proceedings is set out below.
The respondent has not been in paid employment since 2006. She obtained a degree in 2003.
The parties have three children. Mr B is now almost 20. He has Down Syndrome. He is currently living with the respondent, and spending time with the applicant. Mr C is now 18 and attends boarding school in his last year of school. He spends alternate weekends with each of the parties. D is aged 16 and is currently living with her father. She is not spending time with her mother.
The first round of proceedings under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) started in 2013, concluding on 20 June 2014 with consent orders for property division, spousal maintenance and child support. The orders provide for the applicant to purchase a property for $2 million, with title to be held 75 per cent to the respondent and 25 per cent to the applicant. After the orders the parties reached a further agreement, and purchased the Suburb E property for $2,310,000, held 65 per cent by the respondent and 35 per cent by the applicant. The applicant provided the finance for the purchase, predominantly through a mortgage. The orders required the applicant to repay that amount within 10 years.
The applicant was ordered to pay the respondent $1,000 per week in spousal maintenance. The parties entered a binding child support agreement for the applicant to pay $700 per week per child, and to meet private school fees and associated costs, private health insurance cover and medical and dental expenses by way of non-periodic payments.
At the time of the initial orders the applicant’s income was between $17,988 and $21,223 per week.
Further proceedings were commenced by the applicant to set aside the consent orders and child support agreement in 2017 after he defaulted on his obligations with respect to spousal maintenance and child support.
On the first day the matter was listed for final hearing the applicant advised he had filed a Debtor’s Petition in January 2018 and was bankrupt. He had transferred ownership and control of business operations conducted by various companies and trusts to his partner, with whom he worked. He was to be released from the bankruptcy in February 2021. The final hearing was adjourned.
Final parenting orders were made by consent on 6 December 2018 for the three children to live with the respondent. Time was ordered with the applicant for Mr C and D on alternate weekends from Friday to Monday, and for Mr B each alternate Friday to Wednesday. The children shared holiday time with each of their parents. The parties had equal shared parental responsibility.
Following a final hearing lengthy orders were made by Watts J on 25 June 2020. Relevant parts can be summarised as follows. The orders discharged the binding child support agreement and spousal maintenance order, and all arrears under prior orders, agreements and TPDNs were discharged to the date paid.
Pursuant to s 79 of the Act orders were made for the respondent to pay the Trustees in Bankruptcy $315,000 and discharge the Trustees of any liability with respect to the mortgage on the Suburb E property. The Trustees were ordered to contemporaneously transfer to the respondent all their interest in the Suburb E property. Dr O as director of CC Pty Ltd was to do all things necessary to transfer to the respondent all interest held by them in the CC Trust which held units in N Trust, N Pty Ltd and CC Pty Ltd.
An order was made pursuant to s 90XT(1)(b) of the Act for the respondent to take 50 per cent of the interests of the applicant in Q Superannuation Fund, including the interest in the unit trust which owns Unit 2 MN Street Suburb G. The trustee of the superannuation fund, BB Pty Ltd and Dr O were directed to calculate the entitlement to the respondent and pay the entitlement whenever the trustee makes a splitable payment from the respondent’s interest. The orders have effect from the operative time being seven days after service upon the trustee.
An order was made for BB 2 Pty Ltd to transfer to the respondent one half of the shares in that company. Dr O as the director of BB 2 Pty Ltd was to appoint the respondent as a director of the company. In the event that it transpired that BB Pty Ltd does not own the units in the unit trust which owns Unit 2 MN Street, then BB 2 Pty Ltd were to transfer 50 per cent of those units to the respondent.
An order was made for Ms YZ as principal of YZ & Associates to pay to the respondent the whole of the monies in the controlled monies account, with the respondent to be responsible for 51 per cent of any tax liability in respect of the dispositions of the properties at FG Street, Suburb GH and QQ Street, Suburb G and the whole of the responsibility for any capital gains tax liability in respect of the disposition of Unit 1, MN Street, Suburb G and PP Street, Suburb G.
It was ordered that after Dr O’s compliance with all obligations under the property settlement order, any interim order restraining Dr O from dealing with any company or her interest in any company or resigning as director of any company, be discharged.
An order was made that the respondent indemnify the Trustees in respect of any debt she has proved in the applicant’s bankrupt estate.
The orders of Watts J dealt with the arrears of child support and spousal maintenance to the date of the hearing. During his bankruptcy the applicant was to pay child support in the sum of $1,000 per week together with 100 per cent of school fees and costs, and the children’s medical and dental treatment. Once discharged from his bankruptcy, the applicant’s periodic child support payments were to increase to $2,100 per week.
On 27 August 2020 orders for spousal maintenance were amended under the Slip Rule to provide for periodic spousal maintenance of $1,000 per week, adjusted in accordance with CPI after the applicant’s release from bankruptcy. The order continues until D turns 18 or completes her high school certificate, or the respondent earns $52,000 per annum, or remarries or lives in a de facto relationship. Should the respondent earn $30,000 the payment shall reduce pro rata for each dollar earned over that amount.
These proceedings were commenced by the applicant filing an Initiating Application on 23 December 2021 seeking to set aside the child support and spousal maintenance orders and vary parenting orders. On 22 February 2022 the respondent filed an Application for Contempt. The Application was withdrawn in February 2023.
On 14 April 2022 the applicant’s interim application to discharge child support and spousal maintenance orders was dismissed by Justice Schonell following an interim hearing.
In 2023 the respondent commenced proceedings in the Supreme Court of NSW against the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (“CBA”) with respect to the mortgage on the Suburb E property. She seeks to challenge the validity of the mortgage.
On 15 April 2024 the parties consented to final orders that Mr B and Mr C live with and spend time with each parent according to their wishes.
On 14 June 2024 the respondent sought orders under s 102QAC(1)(a) of the Act. That Application came before Curran J on 24 September 2024. On 11 July 2024 the respondent applied for the ICL to be discharged. That application came before Brasch J on 16 July 2024. Both applications were dismissed.
The respondent filed an interim application on 27 August 2024 seeking parenting orders for D. The application was heard by Curran J. Orders were made on 24 September 2024 suspending previous parenting orders from 2018 and providing that D live with, spend time with and communicate with her parents in accordance with her wishes.
D has been living with the applicant since April 2024. She has spent limited time with the respondent since then. She attended Private School 1 from primary school. In early 2024 she began refusing to attend school. She commenced at EK School in Term 4 2024. She missed a significant amount of school in 2024.
D attends on a psychologist, Ms DQ. She has done since August 2024. During the course of proceedings orders were made restraining the parents from communicating with Ms DQ unless requested to do so in writing, and from issuing subpoena to her without leave of the court. This was to preserve for D their therapeutic relationship.
At the outset of the hearing I advised the parties that the affidavits were read without annexures. This is to ensure that only relevant material is tendered. A great deal of documentation was tendered. Much of this I understood to be from the material annexed to the parties’ affidavits.

NCAT proceedings between the applicant and the Health Care Complaints Commission

The applicant was found guilty of unsatisfactory professional conduct in early 2016.
There have been two separate matters brought by the HCCC in the Occupational Division of NCAT. The first was commenced in 2019 when the HCCC sought restrictions on the applicant’s capacity to work as a medical professional due to a history of substance abuse. The other proceedings involved the applicant encouraging patients to invest into his business from late 2015. The first stage of those proceedings was determined in mid-2024, and the second stage in late 2024.
Related to the investment matter, in mid-2022 the applicant was charged with fraud by NSW Police. Ultimately, he was not convicted.
From 2019 drug testing regimes were ordered by NCAT for the applicant. Nineteen tests were requested from September 2019 to March 2020. Eight were reported as too dilute to be relied on, and six requests were not complied with. A hair sample was taken in January 2020 which demonstrated high levels of illicit substances. In mid-2024 the applicant tested positive for cannabinoids on three occasions.
Dr DX was an expert in those proceedings. In a report dated 10 June 2022 (Exhibit 3) he accepted contamination of the hair tested in 2020 could not be ruled out, but opined the extremely high levels and continuation of trace illicit substance present in samples made it highly unlikely the illicit substance was present through contamination alone. He noted in the report the applicant’s demonstrated non-use of substances for close to two years prior to the report.
Dr DX opined the applicant’s lack of insight into his substance use disorder increased the likelihood of relapse. Nonetheless he regarded the applicant as demonstrating good progress from September 2020 in engaging with psychiatric follow up, and urine and hair drug testing. In mid-2023 conditions were placed on the applicant’s medical registration. Further drug testing was conducted on the applicant which were negative for illicit substances.
A further hearing was conducted in mid-2024 before NCAT. This concerned whether the applicant was an impaired medical professional. The hearing was decided in late 2024.
In late 2024 the applicant was disqualified from practising medicine for more than 12 months. He has indicated he does not propose practising medicine in the future.

DOCUMENTS RELIED ON

The parties identified the documents relied on in their Case Outline Documents. A large number of documents were tendered and marked as exhibits. I have had regard to those documents, as well as the material relied upon by each of the parties.
The Court was assisted by a Specific Issues Report prepared by Ms EL (“the report writer”) dated 11 December 2024.
Both parties and the report writer were cross-examined.

COMPETING PROPOSALS

With respect to parenting, the applicant seeks D live with, spend time with and communicate with each parent consistent with her wishes. The ICL supports this order. The applicant does not seek to discharge the previous order from 2018 for equal shared parental responsibility for D.
The ICL also seeks that all other parenting orders be discharged except for Orders 2, 14 and 15 of the orders made by consent on 6 December 2018. Those orders provide for equal shared parental responsibility, non-denigration, and respectful communication between the parties.
The respondent seeks D spend time with her at times to be agreed in writing, in consultation with D. The respondent had sought orders for decision-making authority for D. Decision-making is not referred to in her Minute of Orders tendered on 20 June 2025. I have dealt with decision-making in these reasons to make clear the current situation for D.
With respect to financial proceedings, the applicant seeks orders for child support made on 25 June 2020 be discharged effective from 1 January 2021. Alternatively, that child support be discharged effective from the date of these orders. He seeks an order that he pay child support as assessed, calculated and collected by Services Australia.
The applicant seeks the order for spousal maintenance made on 27 August 2020 be discharged effective from 1 January 2021, or alternatively, from the date of these orders.
The respondent seeks the Court dismiss the applicant’s application. She seeks an order that the applicant deliver D to the ICL so that the orders can be explained to her.
She wants the applicant to be restrained by injunction from causing D to be late to school or to miss any further school.
The respondent seeks an order that the applicant be responsible for any private school fees for D if she attends a private school in 2026, 2027 and/or 2028. She wants to be permitted to attend all school events and liaise with the school in accordance with these orders.
The respondent seeks payment to her by the applicant for fees owing or paid to Private School 4, Private School 5, EM Company, and costs of formal tickets.
She seeks payment of $33,681.78 on account of monies owed to her in relation to the TPDN issued in October 2024 to Services Australia Medicare.
The respondent seeks payment of 50 per cent of any monies received by the applicant in connection with any income from her share of the superannuation asset located at Suburb G.
The respondent seeks an order that any amounts paid to Gannon Family Law and/or EO Lawyers to defeat existing orders of the Court be set aside. Those funds are to cover the listed expenses, or alternatively, the applicant and Mr EJ shall be jointly and severally liable to pay them.
The respondent seeks an order that the Court refer Mr EJ and any other relevant person or party to the Legal Services Commissioner for consideration of initiating disciplinary proceedings against Mr EJ or any other person for his conduct in these proceedings.
The respondent seeks a further payment of $79,576.16 from the applicant and Mr EJ. $25,343.30 is for money in relation to the TPDN issued in December 2021 to DN Group Pty Ltd. The remainder of the funds relate to the respondent’s legal fees.
She seeks an order granting her 28 days to prepare a schedule of any additional disbursements incurred by her from December 2021 to date which the applicant and Mr EJ are liable to pay her.
The respondent seeks the court make a ‘harmful proceedings order’ pursuant to s 102QAC(1)(a) of the Act against the applicant.
The respondent seeks a declaration she has not received her share of the superannuation asset currently occupied by the applicant and previously controlled by Dr O per the order made on 25 June 2020.
The respondent seeks leave to approach the court in relation to the superannuation splitting order made on 25 June 2020.
The respondent seeks leave to make an application to the Court in respect of monies concealed during the applicant’s bankruptcy.
As can be seen, the respondent seeks a variety of orders. She makes a general complaint that previous financial orders have not been complied with by the applicant. There are difficulties with a number of the orders, as I explain in these reasons.
I appreciate that there are many stresses for a litigant representing themselves. The respondent has sought to conduct herself in these proceedings with dignity, and in compliance with court requirements.

PARENTING

THE LAW

Part VII of the Act deals with parenting orders. Section 60CA of the Act provides that the court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration in making a parenting order. Section 60CC(2), (2A) and (3) of the Act set out a list of matters to be considered in determining what is in a child’s best interest. It is not an exhaustive list.
In considering what orders would promote the safety of the child, the authorities of M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69; [1988] HCA 68 and Isles & Nelissen (2022) FLC 94-042 are relevant to any assessment of risk to the child. In a matter where there are allegations of risk such as in this instance, the analysis requires consideration of past conduct to assess whether there is a risk to the child, and the magnitude of that risk. The assessment of risk is an “evidence-based conclusion” (Isles & Nelissen (2022) FLC 94-042 at [85]). The court must then consider whether that risk is capable of being mitigated by imposing restraints or conditions.
I will have regard to relevant matters set out under s 60CC of the Act as they arise relevant to this matter. Although the application relates only to D, understanding the situation for her siblings assists in appreciation of her position. This is particularly so given the changes to care arrangements that have happened for all the children following the final orders in 2018.

THE CHILDREN

Mr B

Mr B has trisomy 21 (Down syndrome). He lived with his mother and spent time with his father until the beginning of 2025, when he moved to live with the applicant. The respondent perceives this occurred due to the applicant’s manipulation of Mr B. She had previously regarded Mr B as immune from manipulation because of his intellectual disability. She found his estrangement from her extremely upsetting.
While the matter was adjourned part heard Mr B returned to the respondent’s home over the Easter long weekend 2025. He has since spent time with the applicant overnight and remains living with his mother.

Mr C

Mr C is now 18 years old and completing his last year of school as a boarder. He has generally lived with his mother post-separation. In 2022 Mr C spent a period living with his father and did not spend time with his mother. The respondent points to this occurring at the same time as the applicant was facing fraud charges. She says he manipulated Mr C to advantage him in those proceedings. The applicant argues this occurred weeks before the criminal trial commenced and was not related.
In 2023 from the beginning of the first school term Mr C resumed spending time with his mother. He boards at school during the school week, alternates weekends with his parents, and school holidays are shared with his parents essentially as previously ordered.

D

D is now 16 years old. Like her siblings, she has been the subject of family law proceedings since 2013. Her parents have been in dispute about arrangements for her and her siblings, and financial matters for the majority of her life.
In April 2024 D commenced living with her father after spending time with him in the holidays. The applicant’s evidence is D told him she needed a break from her mother, and felt stressed.
D has spent limited time with her mother since then. They spent time together briefly at Christmas in 2024, for D’s birthday in 2025, in February 2025, and for D’s birthday in 2025. They communicate by text regularly. D does not generally call, or answer calls from her mother.
On 24 September 2024 interim orders were made for D to live with, spend time with, and communicate with each parent in accordance with her wishes.
The respondent regards each of the children’s periods of estrangement from her as a product of the applicant’s manipulation. The context of his manipulation is to seek advantage in other proceedings.
For example, in 2022 the applicant was charged with fraud, which was listed for hearing in the Local Court. During those proceedings Mr C was living with his father and did not spend time with his mother. The respondent argues this was a strategy by the applicant to portray himself positively as a father to assist in the criminal proceedings. Once the proceedings were over, and Mr C was away from the influence of the applicant at boarding school, his relationship with his mother resumed. He now spends alternate weekends with each of his parents. Her evidence is Mr C contacts her up to three times each week, and asks her to visit him at boarding school. Their close relationship has resumed.
A Single Expert Report was prepared by Ms DT in December 2022. Mr C was then living with the applicant and not spending time with his mother. The report refers to D enjoying living with her mother and spending time regularly with her father, and that arrangement should continue.
A Specific Issues Report was prepared dated 11 December 2024. It provides a different perspective on the mother-daughter relationship than observed by Ms DT in December 2022, as referenced below.

Views

The report writer interviewed D and her father for the Specific Issues Report. The respondent declined to participate in the report. At the time of the interview D was aged 14 years.
D told the report writer she decided to live with her father because she and her mother fight. It was noted at paragraph 22 of the report that there was no particular incident which led to her moving, it just “built up”. She would like to see her mother when she wants. The report writer noted at paragraph 38 she appears to have experienced her mother having unrealistic expectations of her, being controlling, and intruding into her social and school life. The report writer assessed D as likely favouring the parent who provides her with more freedom.
The report writer noted it is not psychologically healthy for D to cut one parent out of her life. The conflict between her parents “allows and encourages her to align with one parent and reject the other”. D wanting not to spend time with her mother appeared “somewhat disproportionate to her experiences with her mother” in the report writer’s estimation.
The respondent raises the timing of D moving to her father’s coincided with the conclusion of Stage 1 of NCAT proceedings commenced by the HCCC. The applicant was seeking to retain his registration to practise medicine. The respondent regards those proceedings as the reason for D moving to live with her father, because the applicant sought an advantage by having her live with him.
The report writer’s assessment of D suggests her views are genuine. D has maintained her views about her living arrangements over a lengthy period. I accept this is not consistent with her being swayed solely by manipulation from her father.
D described her parents’ relationship as “not good”. The report writer noted D appeared to not want to show her distress at that. When asked if she wanted to give a message to the Judge or her parents she said: “I think the Judge should ban them from going back to Family Court” and “this whole thing is ridiculous” (paragraph 35 of the Specific Issues Report).
As indicated by the ICL D has maintained a wish to determine where she lives and time arrangements. She continues to see her psychologist monthly. She is applying for casual work to fit around school. Life for her appears more settled than it was previously.
D has not maintained a relationship with her mother while living with her father. She had poor school attendance while living with him. These are both concerning matters for D now and into the future.
There is a real question of whether orders of this Court would make a difference to either of those matters. She was assessed by the report writer as mature and articulate as her almost 15 years would suggest. She is unlikely to remain in a situation where she does not wish to be. Neither of her parents have demonstrated a capacity to compel her to do something when she has a fixed view not to. I do not say this by way of criticism, but because it is a fact to be taken into account when considering making an order for a young person her age.

D being involved by her parents in these proceedings

The applicant sent email correspondence to Court Child Services when arrangements were being made for an interview with D. The correspondence raised issues for D if her mother was present when she attended for interviews. D was copied into the correspondence by her father. I accept this was not in her interests, and detrimental to her relationship with her mother.
I note both parents and D attended EK School for interviews not long before report interviews were scheduled. There are no reported issues for D. I accept the applicant’s actions were deliberate to negatively influence the relationship between D and her mother, and the report process.

School attendance

D has had issues with school attendance from 2024. In Term 1 her attendance was 71 per cent. From early Term 2 while living with her father she ceased attending. At the time of the hearing her attendance was at 59 per cent. The applicant describes her at times looking unwell and complaining of feeling sick, including loss of appetite. This does not account for her high level of absences.
D has told her father she going to school and then not attended. At times the applicant drove her to school and she subsequently left. School attendance is of vital importance for D’s future, regardless of her relationship with her parents. While the applicant may not have taken all steps possible to ensure her attendance, there is no question that D has decided not to attend school. That is a decision that is not easy to prevent. She is of an age and stage of development where she has capacity to act on decisions she makes.

Safety

Should the applicant abuse substances it would impede his parenting capacity by affecting his judgment, and responsiveness to D’s needs. Substance abuse raises practical concerns of his driving with D in the car. It raises the possibility of a teenager gaining access to substances in the home, and is poor role modelling. I accept the applicant has in the past not complied with testing regimes imposed by NCAT. There have been negative results thereafter.
In August 2024 D told her father she felt sick and had taken six Panadol tablets. He warned her of the risk of liver failure from excessive consumption, and not to take more than eight tablets in a 24-hour period.
In late 2024 D was unwell. When questioned by her father she thought she may have taken nine Panadol tablets. The applicant took her to hospital worried she may have paracetamol poisoning. At hospital D said she took nine tablets over four hours. The applicant told the report writer that she “took too many Panadol trying to treat anxiety”.
The applicant’s evidence is he told hospital staff she may have taken 18 to 20 tablets. He wanted them to take seriously the risk of excess Panadol consumption. Testing confirmed normal liver function, consistent with D’s estimate. At the time of the incident there were concerns this could have been self-harm by D. Neither parent now suggests this is an incident of self-harm. I accept that D did not attempt to harm herself by ingesting Panadol.
The applicant did not advise the respondent about taking D to hospital. His evidence is he spoke to D who indicated it was “my personal medical information”, and she did not want her mother to know. The respondent became aware of the incident through the mother of a friend of D. I accept the applicant should have advised the respondent. Taking a child to hospital for possible overdose of medication is a serious matter, which both parents need to know.
D may be unsafe if required by order to live or spend time with her mother, because she may put herself at physical risk by leaving. She is at risk of psychological harm if she is required by order to remain with her mother against her views. This risk is heightened if there is conflict between them. D has demonstrated the capacity to act on her views previously, by leaving her mother’s home and school. It is likely she would do so again if placed in a situation not of her choosing.

HAS THE APPLICANT MANIPULATED THE CHILDREN TO ASSIST HIM IN LITIGATION?

The respondent describes the applicant as engaging in a “collateral abuse of process” by exploiting the children for his benefit in proceedings including NCAT with respect to his medical registration, the Local Court fraud matter in 2022, and these proceedings. He was dishonest in getting patients to invest in his business, and he has been dishonest in providing information in various jurisdictions about other proceedings. He has not provided disclosure in these proceedings, including of statements relied on in other proceedings. He has not produced documents in accordance with a Notice to Produce. The respondent argues this Court should infer from his lack of disclosure that he has not been honest with this Court.
The respondent has held this view about the applicant’s collateral abuse of proceedings for a long time. She wrote to the applicant’s solicitors and ICL on 30 August 2024:
the interview with [D] [with the newly appointed ICL] is taking place [...] before your client’s stage 2 hearing [at NCAT]. You will recall when your client’s civil NCAT matter was being litigated criminally, [...] before the hearing commenced [Mr C] left my care, [...] weeks to the day [Mr DZ] [previous ICL] advised [Mr C] wished to live with his father, and [...] weeks to the day before I received an application for full custody of [Mr C]. [Ms EP] [ICL], how uncanny. You are involved, too. Shame on you. This will allow [Mr EJ] to be prepared to indicate [D’s] alleged “wishes” to NCAT just in time for the hearing. Lol. You think I’m dumb. All of you. ...
There is no dispute the respondent was the primary carer for all the children before separation. She blames the applicant for causing a fracture in her relationship with Mr C in 2022. The relationship repaired when Mr C started the 2023 school year as a weekly boarder because his time with his father diminished. This lessened the applicant’s influence.
The respondent blames the applicant for manipulating Mr B to live with him at the beginning of 2025. He returned to her care in Easter 2025. Since then, he lives with the respondent and spends time with the applicant.
The Stage 2 NCAT proceedings commenced around one week after D was taken to hospital for ingesting Panadol in late 2024. The respondent submits the applicant exploited this incident as part of his strategy to use D to gain an advantage in proceedings. She argues this is consistent with evidence of Dr DX, psychiatrist and addiction specialist, who in his report to NCAT described the applicant as “interpersonally exploitative ... to achieve his own ends” and lacking in empathy. As set out earlier in these reasons, Dr DX concluded in that report the applicant was making good progress and from 2020 had engaged with psychiatric services and not relapsed into substance abuse. This conclusion is not supportive of the respondent’s case.
The respondent complains the applicant has provided D with cash and permitted extravagant purchases of things like cosmetics and clothing while she has been living with him. The applicant agrees he gave D money as an incentive for school performance. The applicant paid D $300 days before her interview with the ICL on 28 June 2024. The respondent’s position is that purchases and payments were made by the applicant to induce D to live with him. The applicant denies that.
All the children, including D, have been aware of their parents’ conflict over many years. D has been subjected to investigation by DCJ with respect to allegations in 2024 of sexual abuse, which were not made out. As the report writer observed at paragraph 36: “there are significant concerns for [D’s] psychological wellbeing in the context of chronic entrenched conflict between her parents”.
The parents have been preoccupied with their conflict, which I accept has impacted their capacity to care for their children. The respondent’s argument of manipulation and collateral abuse of process by the applicant ignores these complex dynamics.
The report writer noted at paragraph 38 that “[D] appears to have been experiencing conflict in her relationship with her mother when she was living with her”. The affidavit of Ms EQ, the respondent of a school friend of D, notes changes in D’s behaviour during 2024 while in her mother’s care.
I accept the applicant has not assisted D in maintaining her relationship with her mother. I accept he has sought to take advantage of situations as they arose to assist his position at the time. I do not accept that he has orchestrated or manipulated changes in living arrangements for D and the boys over years for the purpose of benefiting him in litigation, in these or other proceedings.
I accept evidence of his family situation and stress from these proceedings has been relied on by him before other courts and tribunals to seek an outcome favourable to him. It would be surprising if those types of submissions were not made on his behalf, where relevant. That does not mean he engineered the children’s situations for that collateral purpose.
The applicant has involved D in the parenting dispute. He agreed not to advise the respondent about serious matters for D in a way which promoted an alignment between father and daughter against the respondent. Not advising the respondent about D attending hospital is an example of that. His evidence is essentially D’s relationship with her mother was up to D. This is not an adequate response to his daughter’s estrangement from her mother.
The respondent relied on a video of D taken in late 2024. The respondent’s lay opinion evidence is D was intoxicated in the video. She was swearing. The respondent referred to a used condom on the ground, visible in the footage. She relied on this as evidence of the applicant’s poor parenting capacity. I am not in a position to determine the reason for D’s conduct. I am confident that her parents’ conflict during most of her life is a factor, for which both parents bear responsibility. I do not accept teenage behaviour, as demonstrated in the video, is necessarily changed by orders of a court.

PARENTING CONCLUSION

Imposing an order on D at 16 years of age carries a risk she will rebel against that. The respondent seeks orders that would require D to spend time with her at times agreed in writing in consultation with D, “consistent with the nature and quantity of time provided by the orders”. It is unclear what is meant by this expression if there is no particular order for time.
The respondent refers to Murati & Roca [2024] FedCFamC2F 1672, where specific orders for time were made for siblings aged 12 and 16, should the mother and children fail to agree on time arrangements. In brief the children did not wish to spend time with the father. Their expressed views aligned with views held by their mother. They had spent time with the father under supervision. Reports from the supervisor demonstrated a relationship between the children and the father different to their expressed views. This decision was determined on its facts, which are distinct from the facts in this matter. For these reasons I do not regard it as helpful in the determination I am required to make.
The respondent’s proposal requires consultation between the respondent and D. There is no evidence D is prepared to do that. This runs the significant risk of further damaging her relationship with her mother, as referred to by the report writer. Orders where the meaning is unclear carries the risk of more litigation and continued conflict with D at the centre.
I accept that leaving the decision on where she lives in D’s hands also carries potential risks to her psychological wellbeing. She may feel pressure from either or both of her parents to comply with their desires and expectations. She could move between the households reactive to situations in either home as they arise, which in itself can present a risk. I note that she has not done that to date. I regard these matters as a lower risk.
I have reached the conclusion that D should be able to live with, spend time with, and communicate with her parents as she wishes. This takes into account a balancing of risks for D. None of the available options are risk free for her. I make these orders on the basis that she will be subjected to less pressure, and in the hope her relationship with her mother may heal. Her brothers spend time in both parents’ households, and she will be able to spend time with each of them. I accept that on balance her best interests, and for safety, will not be met by orders imposed on her against her views.
The applicant seeks there be no order for parental responsibility. The respondent sought a positive order for sole parental responsibility prior to providing a Minute of Orders on 20 June 2026. The ICL seeks the orders for shared parental responsibility remain in place. I provide reasons on this matter when reasons may not be necessary given the respondent’s change of position, to ensure clarity for the parties.
At law making no order means each parent retains parental responsibility, and the ability to make long-term decisions for D. They are required to consult with each other about major long-term decisions, and to have regard to her best interests as the paramount consideration. At 16 years D’s views will be significant in those decisions. I note the parents both attended interviews with her at EK School to facilitate her attendance at that school.
The orders the respondent seeks entitling her to attend school events and engage with the school are not necessary. Both parents are able to engage with the school and attend events. No order being made excludes neither parent. D may have views from time to time about that, which may require the parents to negotiate with her.
I am satisfied issues covered by long-term decision-making such as education, health and religion are mostly resolved for D given her age. She has an ongoing therapeutic relationship with a counsellor, which is agreed should continue. I will discharge the previous orders so that there is no order for parental responsibility.
The respondent seeks an order requiring the applicant to deliver D to the ICL for the purpose of explaining the orders. The ICL has a professional relationship with D. It is for her to organise a meeting with D however they agree that should occur. There is no evidence there has been a particular difficulty in doing so. I will not make the order sought.
The respondent seeks an order restraining the applicant by injunction from causing D to be late to school or to miss school. The applicant has at times driven D to school, and she has left without his knowledge. Ensuring school attendance for D has not been simple. She is engaged with a psychologist. The evidence suggests she has been attending at EK School. While I accept it is in D’s best interest to attend school, I do not regard the order sought as being effective. The order could lead to further litigation over whether the applicant was in breach of the injunction. That would be deleterious to D’s best interests. I will not make that order.
The respondent seeks an order that the applicant be responsible for any private school fees for D if she attends a private school in 2026, 2027 or 2028. There is no current proposal by either party for D to attend a private school. I will not make these orders as there is no evidence either party could meet ongoing private school fees.
The respondent seeks orders permitting her to attend all school events and liaise with the school D attends. The respondent continues to have a role in D’s life. There are no orders that preclude either parent attending at events. I do not propose making an order.
The ICL seeks that all previous parenting orders be discharged except for Orders 2, 14 and 15 of the consent orders made on 6 December 2018, relating to equal shared parental responsibility, non-denigration, and respectful communication between the parties. I have already stated that orders for parental responsibility will be discharged. It is important for D that the parties speak respectfully to each other and refrain from making critical or derogatory remarks. Those orders will remain.

HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS

The respondent seeks the Court make a harmful proceedings order pursuant to s 102QAC of the Act against the applicant. The applicant seeks an order pursuant to s 114(3) of the Act that the respondent be restrained and an injunction issued restraining her from serving the applicant with any application to institute any further proceedings without leave of the court. I am not satisfied that s 114(3) is the appropriate method to moderate further proceedings. Section 114(3) states:
A court exercising jurisdiction under this Act in proceedings other than proceedings to which subsection (1) applies may grant an injunction, by interlocutory order or otherwise (including an injunction in aid of the enforcement of a decree), in any case in which it appears to the court to be just or convenient to do so and either unconditionally or upon such terms and conditions as the court considers appropriate.
The Act has specific provisions for dealing with proceedings which have no prospect of success, or are frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process in Part XIB. A harmful proceedings order falls under a category of orders which requires a party to seek leave before instituting proceedings. The legislation provides matters for the court to consider prior to granting leave. The injunctive power under s 114 does not provide for those matters.
A court may may make a harmful proceedings order if it is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the other party would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party (s 102QAC(1)(a)), or in the case of child-related proceedings, the child who is the subject of the proceedings would suffer harm (s 102QAC(1)(b)).
Neither party addressed this matter particularly in submissions. However, I am satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that each of the parties, and in the case of parenting proceedings D, would suffer psychological and financial harm if the either party were to bring further proceedings. The length of the litigation between them going back at least 12 years speaks to the need for finality for these parties. The respondent was eloquent in expressing her distress at the proceedings.
The order does not ban the parties from bringing further proceedings but provides the court the opportunity to first consider the matter through the leave requirement.
I am satisfied that both parties were given an opportunity to be heard about this as both sought orders with respect to the other. I propose making a harmful proceedings order against both parties.

PROPERTY

LACK OF DISCLOSURE

Each party complains about the other with respect to disclosure. The parties at times sought to rely on past grievances and complaints about the conduct of the other party in previous proceedings. I directed the parties to focus on matters relevant to these proceedings a number of times during the hearing.
The respondent complains there has been inadequate disclosure by the applicant. This has made it difficult for her to argue the case she wishes to make of collateral abuse of proceedings in both the parenting and financial aspects of the matter.
The respondent in submissions complained that the applicant failed to disclose evidence of any monies paid to Dr O in relation to an alleged debt of around $700,000. There is no evidence of money owing to Dr O. I am unable to make a finding about this issue. I raised with the respondent the difficulty in seeking to prosecute matters that reach back before the final orders of Watts J in 2020 on a number of occasions during the hearing.
The respondent complained that the applicant failed to disclose information about his tax, such as tax assessments and returns. She referred to $286,595 in RR Lawyers trust account in 2019, which she was told was being held to pay outstanding tax liabilities. She made reference to ‘bankruptcy crimes’ committed by the applicant. Again, these issues predate the judgment of Watts J.
The applicant says in his Financial Statement that he owes $461,652 for total income assessed and unpaid for the last financial year to the ATO. He provided an ATO accounts summary, undated other than a print date of 28 February 2025. This provides no detail of income nor tax assessed for any period. It shows total income tax owed of $312,631.45, and $507,697.52 debt for activity statement. The applicant has not provided documents which permit an understanding of his current income nor tax liability.
The respondent complained the applicant failed to provide trust account statements from EO Lawyers and Gannon Family Law. She seeks an order that any amounts paid to Gannon Family Law and/or EO Lawyers to defeat existing orders of the Court be set aside. I accept the respondent is highly suspicious of the actions of solicitors’ engaged by the applicant. She believes she has been a victim of concerted efforts to put assets out of reach during the property proceedings before Watts J. As I have indicated, there is no s 79A application to set aside or vary the previous orders. Nor is this an application for enforcement. I cannot take evidence of her concerns of what occurred previously and pursue that into these proceedings without evidence, or an application that supports that enquiry.
The applicant did not file a Financial Statement when requested to do so by the respondent’s then solicitor in December 2020, and again in January 2021. At that time the applicant was not making payments pursuant to orders for spousal maintenance and child support. I accept he should have provided financial disclosure. He has subsequently filed a Financial Statement.
The applicant’s mother gave evidence of a loan of $250,000 made to the applicant in 2017. The funds were obtained by way of a mortgage secured over her property in Town ER, where she resides. The loan was paid into the applicant’s former solicitor’s trust account. The money was used by the applicant to meet payments to creditors. His mother’s evidence is the loan was repaid and the mortgage discharged as at 12 February 2025.
The applicant’s evidence is that he paid his mother in August 2023 $150,000, which finalised the debt. The source of funds is not clear. I accept he prioritised the debt to his mother over payment of ongoing liabilities to the respondent and for the children.
The applicant received a further loan of $100,000 from his mother after he lost his medical licence in late 2024. The money was paid into an CL Bank account opened by the applicant in the UK. He holds UK citizenship. The CL Bank statement shows a payment from the applicant to his mother of 19,937.65 GBP on 19 December 2024. There is a further payment to his mother of 14,000 GBP on 6 February 2025. The applicant’s evidence is he returned 12,000 GBP to his mother and retained the rest of those funds. This again demonstrates the difficulty in relying on assertions of the applicant.
The applicant gave evidence he intended using the funds he retained for D’s education. There is no evidence he has done so, particularly as she is now attending a public school with limited expenses.
It was suggested the applicant has used funds in the CL Bank account for trading. Statements from the CL Bank account were not disclosed to the respondent until the commencement of the final hearing after she served a Notice to Produce. These were documents the applicant was obliged to produce pursuant to the rules for disclosure.
The respondent argues that the applicant has used his mother’s bank accounts to conceal funds and send the money offshore.
The Financial Statement filed by the applicant on 7 February 2025 refers to personal loans of $215,000 from Mr CQ. Mr CQ is the applicant’s brother-in-law. The Financial Statement makes no reference to any debt to his mother.
The applicant gives no evidence about the basis of the debt to Mr CQ. He provides no documents disclosing a loan document, payment of loan monies, nor affidavit from Mr CQ.
The respondent states in an addendum to her Financial Statement that the applicant began making regular payments to Mr CQ from at least 2020. There is no evidence from the applicant to show how much is outstanding to Mr CQ, nor how much has been paid.
I accept that the applicant has failed to make proper disclosure of financial records to the respondent. Set out above are numerous examples of his failure to do so.
The respondent makes a complaint that a decision by NCAT in late 2024 to cancel or suspend the applicant’s medical registration was not disclosed to her. She relied on correspondence from late 2024 where she sought disclosure, which was not responded to. The applicant made no admission about this, despite the issue being raised during the hearing, until final submissions. I note that the applicant was self-represented in late 2024. When his solicitor was again on the record, the judgment was still not disclosed.
There is no dispute the applicant has not paid child support or spousal maintenance in accordance with the orders of Watts J. His evidence is the orders were impossible for him to meet. He denied having no intention of paying the orders. The applicant was cross-examined about choosing to pay other debts, including to his mother, rather than pay school fees. He gave evidence the respondent was able to pay the school fees and his mother needed the security. This ignores that it was his liability pursuant to the orders.
I accept the applicant’s attitude throughout his evidence was that it was not his responsibility. This is reflected in his Financial Statement where he does not record his liability pursuant to the orders for school fees, nor his liability from his failure to make payments in accordance with child support and spousal maintenance orders of Watts J.
The applicant has not fulfilled his obligations with respect to disclosure. I accept there can be no doubt given his experience in litigation in this court he was aware of those obligations. Those obligations fall on him, not legal practitioners who act for him.
The applicant complains the respondent has failed to provide adequate disclosure to him of the proceedings in the Supreme Court of NSW with the CBA.
The trust account ledger for the respondent’s solicitors in the Supreme Court proceedings shows deposits made on 8 December 2022 of $150,000 described as “settle monies/legal costs” from which legal fees were paid. On 30 May 2023 $600,000 was deposited described as settlement monies. I accept the respondent did not disclose this amount to the applicant when the funds were received. In September 2024 $361,836.07 stood in the trust account.
The respondent provided evidence in her affidavit of 11 February 2025, that the Supreme Court proceedings settled for $750,000. She paid her solicitors $141,103.94. She paid counsel’s fees in an undisclosed amount. She used some funds for an operation to reduce weight. That amount was not disclosed.
The respondent provided a list of her assets and liabilities in her Case Outline filed 28 February 2025. She held assets of $4,110,646.69 and liabilities of $2,601,081.93 inclusive of a mortgage to the CBA of $2,313,929. It is unclear what the current status of the mortgage is given the litigation in the Supreme Court.
I accept in keeping with the authorities, Weir and Weir [1992] FamCA 69; (1993) FLC 92-338 (“Weir”), I should not be unduly cautious of making findings in favour of the innocent party. Both parties have, at times, not met their obligations. I accept non-disclosure by the applicant has been far more significant in his case. As a consequence of his failure to disclose his financial position is opaque.

SUPERANNUATION

The respondent sought various orders with respect to superannuation. The orders of Watts J divided the parties’ self-managed superfund as to 50 per cent each. The orders required the respondent be appointed as a director to BB 2 Pty Ltd. That entity owns the Suburb G property which is the main asset of the fund.
The respondent seeks a declaration she has not received her share of superannuation assets pursuant to the splitting order. She seeks “leave to approach the Court in relation to the superannuation splitting Order made on 25 June 2020 and related matters”. She seeks from 6 March 2025 to date of the orders payment by the applicant of 50 per cent of any monies received by him from income generated by the respondent’s share of the superannuation asset at Suburb G.
The respondent’s affidavit refers to Dr O instructing RR Lawyers to send her documents to sign on 24 July 2020 to make her the director of BB 2 Pty Ltd. She did not sign the documents because “I did not know the liabilities of that company, or [BB Pty Ltd], as [Dr O] and [Mr Porter] did not disclose the companies’ liabilities to me during the substantive proceedings. I also did not trust [RR Lawyers] for reasons I elaborate below”. She was subsequently forwarded by the applicant a final warning from the ATO addressed to the Trustee for FF Trust. On the evidence it is unclear what FF Trust is, and the relevant trustees. The evidence only discloses that BB Pty Ltd is the trustee of Q Superannuation Fund. BB 2 Pty Ltd owns the property at Unit 2 MN Street, Suburb G. The orders of Watts J make no reference to FF Trust.
There is no clear explanation from the respondent about her decision not to sign documents appointing her as director of BB 2 Pty Ltd, pursuant to the orders. There is no evidence of a debt attached to that entity.
Under s 79A(1) of the Act the court may vary or discharge property orders if the court is satisfied, relevantly:
(a) there has been a miscarriage of justice by reason of fraud, duress, suppression of evidence (including failure to disclose relevant information), the giving of false evidence or any other circumstance; or

(b) in the circumstances that have arisen since the order was made it is impracticable for the order to be carried out or impracticable for a part of the order to be carried out; or

(c) a person has defaulted in carrying out an obligation imposed on the person by the order and, in the circumstances that have arisen as a result of that default, it is just and equitable to vary the order or to set the order aside and make another order in substitution for the order; or

...
The respondent in her affidavit seeks she be appointed trustee for sale of the Suburb G property. She does not seek that on her Minute of Orders nor Amended Response. It is difficult to understand with clarity the order she seeks.
The application that she makes is based on little evidence about the superannuation fund. I have no documents tendered relating to the fund. I do not have documents which demonstrate whether the respondent was appointed as director of BB 2 Pty Ltd. The Application and Response do not seek to vary or set aside previous property orders pursuant to s 79A.
Dr O was a party to the previous proceedings. She is required to calculate the respondent’s entitlement and pay the entitlement whenever the trustee makes a splittable payment from the respondent’s interest in the fund. The respondent acknowledged she had not served Dr O with her Response in these proceedings. She suggested that “botched” service was deliberate on the part of the person serving the documents. I do not accept the evidence provided by the respondent demonstrated a deliberate failure, suggesting collusion between that person and Dr O, and possibly the applicant. The respondent provides no evidence of service. Dr O was not joined on that basis. She has not been provided with notice of these proceedings.
The state of the evidence and lack of service on Dr O means I cannot make orders sought by the respondent with respect to superannuation. Orders in accordance with that part of the respondent’s Response will be not be made.

THIRD PARTY DEBT NOTICES

The respondent has issued TPDNs between October 2020 and October 2024 on about 40 occasions. Through this method she has been able to intercept payments otherwise due to the applicant through Medicare and other sources.
The respondent seeks an order that the applicant and Mr EJ be jointly and severally liable to pay her $25,343.30, plus pre-judgment interest, on account of the balance of monies owed to her in relation to the Third Party Debt Notice (“TPDN”) issued in December 2021 to DN Group Pty Ltd.
Division 11.1.4 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) deal with TPDNs. The Rules provide that following service the third party debtor a debt due or accruing to the debtor is bound in the hands of the third party debtor to the extent specified in the TPDN. The TPDN remains in force until the total amount referred to is paid or the TPDN is set aside.
The TPDN sought payment of $93,415 for monies owed from the orders of Watts J. The payment sought appears to apply to debts related to spousal maintenance, periodic and non-periodic child support.
The applicant filed an Amended Initiating Application on 28 January 2022 seeking interlocutory orders for a stay and discharge of the TPDN directed to DN Group Pty Ltd, and discharge of the orders for spousal maintenance and periodic child support. He sought an order for payment of child support in accordance with the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 (Cth) (“the Assessment Act”).
On 14 April 2022 Schonell J ordered that the applicant do all things to cause DN Group Pty Ltd to pay to the respondent $39,756.70 in satisfaction of the TPDN. There is no evidence this amount is owing. I infer from her material the money was paid.
Justice Schonell noted in his reasons that with the consent of the parties during the course of the interim proceedings orders were made releasing $50,000 to the respondent from DN Group Pty Ltd, to pay outstanding school fees to Private School 1 and Private School 5. The respondent gives evidence:
Justice Schonell ordered, of the funds held with [DN Group Pty Ltd] pursuant to the TPDN, that the Children’s private schools receive $50,000.00, which I split between [Mr C] and [D’s] schools ($20,840.00 and $29,160.00 respectively), and that I receive $39,756.70, and the rest could be dealt with at a final hearing. Given [Mr Porter] owed me $21,000.00 in spousal maintenance and $44,100.00 in child support, I maintain an amount of $25,343.30 remains owing to me.
The respondent calculates she was owed $21,000 in spousal maintenance and $44,100 in child support, and $25,343.30 remains owing to her on account of the TPDN issued in December 2021 to DN Group Pty Ltd. This cannot be correct.
As I understand the evidence the TPDN sought payment of $93,415 from DN Group Pty Ltd. The respondent received $89,756.70: $50,000 for school fees and $39,756.70 Schonell J made orders for specific payments in satisfaction of the TPDN. That is, the TPDN is effectively discharged after payment of the amounts specified in Schonell J’s orders. I do not propose making the order sought by the respondent for payment of $25,343.30. I any event that amount could not have been outstanding under the TPDN to DN Group Pty Ltd.
The respondent also seeks an order that the applicant pay her $33,681.78, plus pre-judgment interest, on account of monies owing in relation to a TPDN issued in October 2024 to Services Australia Medicare. The respondent says that has not been collected and paid in part or in full, and that that was confirmed by a representative at Services Australia.
I propose making orders as sought by the respondent that that TPDN be discharged. The applicant has not practised medicine for some time which may explain the lack of payment by Medicare. That amount is owed by the applicant. I will order he pay the respondent $33,681.78 with respect to money owed pursuant to the TPDN.

SPOUSAL MAINTENANCE

Final orders in the financial matter were made by Watts J on 25 June 2020. The matter was previously listed in January 2018 and adjourned due to the applicant’s bankruptcy. The applicant has maintained he is unable to meet the payments required pursuant to the orders. The respondent has issued TPDNs to obtain payments directly from Medicare.
Section 83(1) of the Ac t provides relevantly that if an order is in force for spousal maintenance the court may subject to s 111AA:
(c) discharge the order if there is any just cause for doing so;

(d) suspend its operation wholly or in part and either until further order or until a fixed time or the happening of some future event;

(e) revive wholly or in part an order suspended under paragraph (d); or

(f) subject to subsection (2), vary the order so as to increase or decrease any amount ordered to be paid or in any other manner.
The applicant seeks to discharge the spousal maintenance order as and from either 1 January 2021, or in the alternate from the date of the orders. 1 January 2021 is the date he was discharged from bankruptcy.
Following his discharge from bankruptcy he continued to work and receive an income. His evidence is from March 2019 he has had a condition imposed by the professional body that he could not work more than 30 hours per week. Later he has had his licence suspended for various periods culminating with a suspension of more than 12 months from late 2024.
The applicant’s evidence of his income in his affidavit is $145,999 for the year ending 2019, $134,278 for the year ending 2020, $474,830 for the year ending 2021, and $595,812 for the year ending 2022. At the time of filing his affidavit on 19 December 2024 he has not filed any other tax returns. He has provided no proper explanation for that failure. He has not provided any detail of income earnt since.
The applicant has largely failed to voluntarily comply with orders for payment of spousal maintenance since the orders of August 2020. He made sporadic child support payments, and none since mid-2022. Payments have been received by the respondent through her seeking the court issue TPDNs.
The applicant’s evidence is that he is currently without an income, save for investment dividends of $300 per week and government benefits. He has investments of $55,432 as listed in his Financial Statement. He has not provided details about his investments.
The onus rests on the applicant to demonstrate just cause for discharging the order. Just cause must be interpreted in the context of the Act as a whole, and particularly s 72 and s 75, being the specific provisions which relate to maintenance: Vakil & Vakil (1997) FLC 92-743; Lutzke & Lutzke [1979] FamCA 60; (1979) FLC 90-714.
Section 72 of the Act provides that a party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other to the extent they are reasonably able to do so, only if the other is unable to support themselves adequately due to care of a child under 18, physical or mental incapacity for gainful employment, or any other adequate reason.
The applicant’s failure to disclose makes it impossible to assess his available funds. I accept that he has not been transparent with respect to disclosure of records relating to the debt to Mr CQ. His evidence is he prioritised paying a debt to his mother over obligations under orders to pay school fees and child support. He has not provided adequate disclosure on the use of either of the two loans from his mother, nor what monies are still available. He failed to disclose until the hearing the CL Bank account, in which he placed funds from his mother’s account.
The applicant argues he is not able to support the respondent, and she is reasonably able to support herself. She does not have care of a child, nor any other adequate reason that would sustain continuation of the order.
In terms of the matters under s 75(2) of the Act the applicant argues he suffers from poor health based on a variety of psychological conditions such as anxiety, depression, ADHD and PTSD. These psychological impediments impacted his ability to practise medicine. From 2019 he has had restrictions on his licence to practise medicine and was not able to do so more than 30 hours per week.
Notwithstanding these issues the evidence is he was earning in excess of $479,000 at the time of Schonell J’s judgment in April 2022.
From late 2024 the applicant’s medical licence was suspended on an interim basis. In late 2024 final orders were made suspending him from practising medicine for more than 12 months. The applicant’s evidence is he does not propose returning to practise at the conclusion of his period of suspension.
The applicant has suffered, I accept, considerable reputational damage with respect to his ability to practise medicine as a medical professional, or in any capacity. I accept there are a number of matters likely to impact on the cost of insurance required for him to practise, such as the proceedings brought by the HCCC, and that he is listed as an impaired medical professional.
The applicant is retraining in the tech industry through ES University. While that occurs, he is in receipt of social security. I accept he has retained some of the money borrowed from his mother noting the difficulties with disclosure.
I accept the suspension of his licence to practise medicine in late 2024 for more than 12 months has been difficult for the applicant for more than financial reasons. While the respondent is concerned that circumstances have been manipulated by the applicant to shirk his responsibilities, I have had regard to the proceedings before NCAT. To the extent the respondent has argued the applicant has manipulated those proceedings in concert with these proceedings to achieve his desired outcome, I reject that argument.
The applicant did not set about having his licence to practise medicine restricted, and then removed, to limit his income. I have no doubt that the unravelling of problems with his practise and substance abuse revealed through the NCAT proceedings was not a course the applicant would voluntarily have adopted. Those proceedings have had a psychological impact on the applicant beyond the impact of his inability to practice in his chosen profession. He has been diagnosed with depression and anxiety. I accept that was not sought by him as part of a collateral process.
The applicant continued to earn an income as a medical professional after he was discharged from bankruptcy. The bankruptcy discharged him of obligations towards his creditors. As evidenced by the TPDNs issued by the respondent, he continued to receive an income. I do not accept that his liabilities should be discharged from that date. He has not provided evidence that supports that conclusion. I propose making orders that discharge the spousal maintenance orders as and from the date of these orders, rather than the alternate order sought by the applicant from 1 January 2021.
The reason for discharge from the date of the orders is the applicant’s lack of disclosure. He has not given evidence of the amount owed pursuant to the orders, and the specific dates he ceased paying. I accept that there have been periods he has met at least some of his obligations, and periods he has not. The respondent has obtained 40 TPDNs to recover funds owed. The amounts received by her from each of those notices is not clear. It is his obligation when applying to discharge the order to make clear the consequences of so doing. I accept that since his licence to practise medicine was suspended there would be an array of difficulties in him taking up practice in mid-2027. It is not yet known what his income will be from work in the tech industry once study is concluded.
The applicant raises the respondent’s ability to earn an income. I accept the respondent is clearly a bright and capable person. She demonstrated her capability through these proceedings, where she has been tenacious. Against that she has been out of the workforce for 20 years. This is a considerable hurdle. She gave evidence about her desire to start an online brand of teenage clothing, given her experience with the children and their friends. Acting for herself in these proceedings has taken a great deal of her time and energy which has limited her opportunity, I accept, to pursue this interest. She also requires some initial capital.
I accept the relative financial position of the parties’ favours the respondent in that she has possession of assets estimated by her in her Financial Statement to be over $4 million, notwithstanding liabilities of $2,601,08.16. The applicant has superannuation which he estimates at $550,000 and $84,923 in other assets as listed in his Financial Statement.
Each party holds 50 per cent of superannuation pursuant to the orders of Watts J. One asset of the superannuation fund is the property at Suburb G. The current value of that property is not known. The respondent has not signed documents that would have given her some control over the assets of the fund. She has sought leave to bring an application with respect to the superannuation fund. There is no question that she is entitled to 50 per cent of the fund. The orders made make provision for transfer of 50 per cent to her.
I propose making s 102QAC orders which will require either party to seek leave prior to making an application. That means each party will be required to satisfy a leave requirement prior to launching further proceedings.
The applicant relies on the duty of the court to end financial relations between parties pursuant to s 81 as a basis for the spousal maintenance order to be discharged. Having made the findings above it is not necessary for me to rule on this aspect of the application. I note however that the orders for spousal maintenance made by Watts J are not open ended. The orders expire when D attains the age of 18, that is, in two years’ time.
Orders for spousal maintenance will be discharged as and from 27 March 2026.

THE CHILD SUPPORT APPLICATION

The applicant seeks to discharge the child support orders made by Watts J on 25 June 2020 from 1 January 2021, or alternatively from the date of these orders. He proposes child support be assessed, calculated and collected in accordance with the Assessment Act.
The applicant relies on s 66S of the Act as providing the court the power to vary a child support order. Section 66S relates to orders for child maintenance. The Assessment Act applies to those children born on or after the date of commencement of the Act, being 1 October 1989 (s 19 of the Assessment Act). The applicable law to vary or discharge the orders is the Assessment Act.
Justice Watts set aside the parties’ binding child support agreement pursuant to s 136 of the Assessment Act. He then made orders for payment of periodic and non-periodic payment of child support. Those orders can, pursuant to s 116, be departed from as though from an administrative assessment.
Order 23 made on 25 June 2020 is a departure order for periodic payments to the respondent. The Court has jurisdiction under s 141(1)(j) of the Assessment Act to vary or discharge a departure order (Gilmour & Gilmour (1995) FLC 92-591). Such an order may be varied provided the applicant can satisfy the Court of a ground for departure, and that is just and equitable and otherwise proper to do so (Rosati & Rosati [1998] FamCA 38; (1998) FLC 92-804).
The applicant has provided no evidence of service of the application on the Child Support Registrar. Rule 1.13 of the Rules requires that applications under the Assessment Act, other than for leave to appeal from an order of a court, must be served on the Child Support Registrar. The Registrar has a right to intervene in proceedings before the court pursuant to s 145 of the Assessment Act.
I note the same issue arose earlier in these proceedings before Schonell J. At [41] he cited Lesley v Lesley [2015] FamCA 894 of McClelland J as he then was. Unfortunately, that judgment remains applicable on the available evidence.
In Child Support Registrar & Nixon,[1] the Full Court said:
... given the emphasis placed by the High Court in both Taylor and Allesch v Maunz on the right of a party to be heard when an order is to be made affecting that party, we accept that where a party has not been given notice of the proceedings in which the order was made, and thus not been heard on the making of the order, that that is a matter which should be given very significant weight in the exercise of the discretion to set aside the order. As his Honour did not refer to the fact that the order had been made without notice to the Registrar, it has to be assumed that he gave this fact no weight, and thus his discretion must be regarded as having miscarried on account of his failure to have regard to this important matter.

We add that we do not accept, as we understood to be submitted by the respondents, that a distinction should be drawn between a party to the proceedings as opposed to a third party. ...
Pursuant to Rule 1.31 of the Rules the court may, in the interests of justice, dispense with compliance, or full compliance, with any of the Rules at any time. I propose exercising the discretion available to deal with child support, notwithstanding the lack of evidence of service.
I do so because D has been living with the applicant since April 2024. She has spent limited time with her mother since then. In those circumstances it is difficult to imagine a prejudice arising to the Registrar of the Child Support Agency in not having the opportunity to be heard.
I accept D living full-time with the applicant, and spending no overnight time with the respondent, is a special circumstance which makes it just and equitable and otherwise proper for me to discharge the order for periodic payments by the applicant to the respondent for D. To do otherwise ignores the reality of D’s living arrangements where all her day-to-day expenses are paid by the applicant. I propose discharging the order from 30 June 2024. The precise date of D’s move to her father full-time is not clear on the evidence. It was during second term 2024. Doing the best I can she was I accept living with her father and spending no time with her mother from 30 June 2024.
Order 23 of the 25 June 2020 orders includes payment by the applicant of various expenses for the children that are non-periodic. Under s 129 of the Assessment Act a court may discharge, suspend, revive, or vary an order previously made for non-periodic child support. Section 129(2) of the Assessment Act provides a court must not make an order under s 129(1) unless it is satisfied it would be:
(a) just and equitable as regards the child, carer entitled to child support and the liable parent concerned; and

(b) otherwise proper;

to make the order.
Section 129(3) relevantly provides a court cannot vary the order under 129(1), unless it is satisfied:
(a) that making the variation is justified because of a change in the circumstances of the child, the carer entitled to child support or a liable parent concerned since the order was made or last varied; or

(c) that making the variation is justified because of a change in the cost of living since the order was made or last varied;

...
Section 129(5) requires the court to have regard to s 117(4), (6), (7), (7A) and (8) of the Assessment Act in determining whether it would be just and equitable as regards the child, payer or payee to make an order under 129(1). Section 129(6) of the Assessment Act requires the court to have regard to s 117(5) in determining whether it would be otherwise proper to make an order under s 129(1).
School fees have not been paid by the applicant pursuant to his obligation under the orders of 25 June 2020, where he is responsible for 100 per cent of the fees and other costs once he is discharged from bankruptcy. Mr C commenced at Private School 5 in 2021. He is in his last year at school. The respondent has paid the Private School 1 school fees for D from 2017 to 2024 through monies received from property settlement and payments from the applicant through TPDNs. The applicant met some payments voluntarily.
The non-periodic orders are framed without reference to continuing after any of the children attain the age of 18 years. I accept that with respect to periodic payments attaining 18 years is a child support terminating event. That means that payment for Mr C and Mr B ceased at the time they turned 18 years as no provision was made in the order for child support to extend to the conclusion of secondary school.
The orders for non periodic payments specify the applicant’s liability for 100 per cent of the fees for schooling and other educational expenses. Logically this continues as long as those fees and expenses are required. Mr C and Mr B were at school at the time of Watts J’s orders. I accept that it would have been contemplated that those fees and expenses would continue after they turned 18 years provided they remained at school.
There are outstanding school fees from 2025 owed to Mr C’s school of $62,320.50. This is a debt to the applicant pursuant to the orders for child support of Watts J made 25 June 2020. In keeping with the applicant’s attitude to disclosure, he makes no reference to debt with respect to school fees in his Financial Statement. Both parties agree Mr C should continue to attend at his current school until he concludes his education at the end of 2026.
