The applicant is liable for all fees up until discharge as at the date of these orders. Thereafter the parties share liability for the remainder of 2026.

Just and equitable

I have had regard to s 4 of the Assessment Act in making my decision. That sets out the objects of the Act, which is to ensure children receive a proper level of financial support from their parents. Parents should provide for their children according to their capacity to provide financial support.
I propose discharging Order 23 of Watts J’s orders dated 25 June 2020 as it relates to payment for periodic child support for D, as and from 30 June 2024.
I propose discharging Order 23 of those orders as it relates to payment for non-periodic child support, as and from 27 March 2026.
I accept that this leaves the issue of Mr C’s school year post these orders to be met by the parties. The applicant has demonstrated the ability to borrow money from friends and his mother. I note each party wishes Mr C to remain at school until the end of the year.

PAYMENT OF INTEREST

The respondent has sought as part of her orders payment of interest. Pursuant to s 117B of the Act interest is payable from the date an order is made, or the date the order comes into effect, on the amount unpaid. That is the case for all orders for payment unless it is specified interest is not payable. I do not propose altering the rate of interest prescribed by the rules.
I do not propose making orders for interest which may interfere with the respondent’s statutory entitlement to claim interest payments.

COMPLAINTS ABOUT THE APPLICANT’S SOLICITOR

The respondent seeks various orders relating to the applicant’s solicitor, Mr EJ. In a matter where parties have been in significant dispute over many years it is to be anticipated there may be hostility and suspicion which extends to legal representatives. When one party has had the same solicitor over a period, it is unsurprising they are identified with their client by an opposing self-represented litigant. That does not mean they act in breach of their professional duty to further their client’s case.
The orders directed to the applicant’s solicitors and former solicitors seeking to recover funds paid to defeat existing orders and costs are not orders I can consider making. There is no evidence that supports the making of such an order. The respondent has grievances with the legal representatives.
Solicitors file Notices of Ceasing to Act for a variety of reasons. It can be issues with fees, a client not providing instructions, a lack of confidence in representation, for example. Solicitors go on and off the record for clients for any number of reasons. Nothing in this suggests a conspiracy between solicitor and client to act improperly.
The respondent complains in her affidavit, for example, that the applicant filed Applications and Amended Applications without an accompanying affidavit. The Rules provide whether an affidavit is to be filed with an application. The Rules have varied over the years these parties have been in litigation. Unless an interim or interlocutory order is sought in an Initiating Application pursuant to the Rules there is no provision for an affidavit to be filed with an Initiating Application.
The respondent seeks the Court refer Mr EJ to the Legal Services Commissioner. She complains that he has made the proceedings more lengthy and costly. She says he has acted without regard for his duty to the court. Her argument is that the solicitor is in breach r 21.1.4 of the Legal Profession Uniform Law Australian Solicitors’ Conduct Rules 2015 (NSW) that “a solicitor must take care to ensure that the solicitor’s advice to invoke the corrective powers of a court is not given principally in order to gain some collateral advantage for the client ... out of court”. This is in keeping with her belief that the applicant is using proceedings in this Court as part of an overarching plan for his advancement in other proceedings.
I have found the applicant has not complied with his obligations of disclosure. Unfortunately, this occurs before courts every day. For fault to lie with the solicitor and not the client who instructs them in my view requires more than that they continue to act for a client who is in breach of orders and or obligations arising under the Act.
The respondent has complained that the applicant engineered a situation where Mr C was living with the applicant shortly prior to the criminal proceedings in August 2022, and that his solicitor colluded with his client to bring about that situation. She was concerned the previous ICL was a friend of the applicant’s solicitor, and under that influence was not fulfilling his role as an ICL. She alleges the applicant’s solicitor colluded with the ICL and the respondent’s solicitors to disadvantage the respondent. There is no evidence of any of those things. An allegation the solicitor has backdated correspondence is made with reference to any factual matter.
The respondent complains the solicitor has permitted the applicant to file misleading affidavits such as the affidavit of 19 December 2024 where the applicant gave evidence Brasch J dismissed the harmful proceedings application brought by the respondent. As I understand the history of proceedings it was dismissed by Curran J on 24 September 2024. An error about which Judge made an order is not conduct requiring a referral to the Legal Service Commissioner.
The respondent makes complaint the applicant’s solicitor:
commits abuse by proxy by serving documents on me again and again at around 5pm knowing I find it upsetting, and repeatedly telling me to get legal advice when he knows I cannot afford it. [Mr EJ] is a perpetrator of family violence and finds great humour in my suffering, which causes our children to suffer.
There is no evidence in support of what is in effect a submission in the respondent’s affidavit.
Family violence as defined in the Act at s 4AB means violent, threatening or other behaviour that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family, or causes the family member to be fearful. This cannot apply to the applicant’s solicitor.
There is no evidence of misconduct by the solicitor that would warrant a referral by me to the NSW Legal Services Commissioner. I note that a complaint can be made to the NSW Legal Services Commissioner by members of the public. The respondent may take that course if she chooses.

COSTS

The respondent seeks that Mr EJ and the applicant be jointly and severally liable to pay $48,034.79 to the respondent, being various costs of these proceedings, and seeks leave to prepare a schedule of further costs. As I have made clear I do not accept that the applicant’s solicitor has engaged in conduct alleged by the respondent. I note that provisions of s 114UB with respect to costs have the general rule that each party bears their own costs. Costs applications are dealt with after delivery of judgment, given the relevant considerations under the Act.
The respondent seeks leave to make an application to the Court in respect of money she says was concealed during the applicant’s bankruptcy, that is before the orders of Watts J. I do not propose granting leave. I note I have referred to making a 102QAC order, so that any further application would require leave. There appears to be no basis on the present evidence to ground such an application.

OTHER PAYMENTS SOUGHT BY THE RESPONDENT FROM THE APPLICANT

The respondent seeks the applicant pay her for various expenses at orders 4(b) to (g) of her Minute of orders relating to Mr B and Mr C’s schooling which she paid. Her evidence about that was not challenged. The expenses relate to school fees, textbooks and other school related expenses. For example, she seeks $6,631 on account of monies she repaid a friend who paid Private School 5 what was owing for Mr C’s 2024 school fees. There is no question the liability for the expenses pursuant to Order 23 of Watts J orders remain with the applicant. On that basis I will make the orders for payment by the applicant of $14,767.69.
The respondent also seeks the applicant pay her $3,244 owing to St Edmund’s College for Mr B’s 2024 school fees and $62,320.50 owing to St Ignatius for Mr C’s 2025 fees. I note she refers to a liability of $3,744 owed to Private School 4 in her Financial Statement, rather than $3,244. There is no issue that the applicant is liable for school fees up to the date of these orders. The applicant remains liable to pay these amounts to the schools. I will note the money owed as detailed by the respondent for ease of reference. There is no issue this is a liability of the applicant.
The respondent seeks payment by the applicant of $63,946.00 on account of 25 payments of $1,157.84 on account of spousal maintenance payments and 25 payments of $1,400 on account of periodic child support payments for the period 14 September 2024 to 6 March 2025. The applicant concedes owing her these amounts. I will fix the amount owed with respect to spousal maintenance to make clear the current debt to be paid by the applicant. This is in keeping with discharging the order for child support from 30 June 2024.
